title: Stolen Sweets
description: Stolen Sweets is a fiction by Charles Paul de Kock. Found indecent in 1917 by a Magistrate's Court under the Indecent Publications Act 1910.
descriptionParagraphs: Stolen Sweets is a fiction by Charles Paul de Kock. Found indecent in 1917 by a Magistrate's Court under the Indecent Publications Act 1910.

Its interest lies partly in the way literary or informational writing gets collapsed into a public-morality problem. As a fiction, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in New Zealand. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: Found indecent in 1917 by a Magistrate's Court under the Indecent Publications Act 1910. The Magistrate S.E. McCarthy held the opinion that wide dissemination of the books "would tend to generate libidinous desires. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

overview.englishDescription: Stolen Sweets is a fiction by Charles Paul de Kock. Found indecent in 1917 by a Magistrate's Court under the Indecent Publications Act 1910.
banningParagraphs: Stolen Sweets entered censorship debates as a fiction associated with morality, print scandal, and sexuality. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around obscenity and public morality.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1917 in New Zealand, where Magistrate's Court classified, prohibited, or restricted. Found indecent in 1917 by a Magistrate's Court under the Indecent Publications Act 1910. The Magistrate S.E. McCarthy held the opinion that wide dissemination of the books "would tend to generate libidinous desires. Found indecent in 1917 by a Magistrate's Court under the Indecent Publications Act 1910. The Magistrate S.E. McCarthy held the opinion that wide dissemination of the books "would tend to generate libidinous desires, and these desires not infrequently prove.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: Literature Suppressed on Sexual Grounds
counterReadings.author: Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: Surveys the legal and moral language used to suppress books as obscene.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for seeing how obscenity law and censorship habits changed over time.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.
